date.gregorianHint: Occurs on the 12th of every Ethiopian month

summary.short: Monthly commemoration of Archangel Michael, protector and intercessor.
summary.panel: The 12th day of each Ethiopian month remembers Archangel Michael and his protection of the faithful.
summary.whyItMatters: The Church honors Michael as a mighty servant of God, defender of the righteous, and helper of the faithful.
summary.connection: It connects believers to God’s protection, angelic ministry, and the victory of divine righteousness.

content.extended: The 12th day of each Ethiopian month remembers Archangel Michael and his protection of the faithful.

expandedContent.whyCelebrated: The 12th day of each Ethiopian month is kept in honor of Archangel Michael, a mighty servant of God and intercessor for the faithful.
whatHappened: The Church remembers Archangel Michael as protector and defender by God's command.
This monthly remembrance keeps his intercession close to the prayer life of the faithful.
The day is commonly marked with prayer, hymns, thanksgiving, and acts of charity.
expandedContent.significance: This commemoration points believers to God's protection and to the ministry of the holy angels in the life of the Church.

date.gregorianHint: Occurs on the 19th of every Ethiopian month

summary.short: Monthly commemoration of Archangel Gabriel, messenger of divine glad tidings.
summary.panel: The 19th day of each Ethiopian month remembers Archangel Gabriel and his ministry of holy announcement.
summary.whyItMatters: It keeps before the faithful the joy of divine message and God’s saving intervention.
summary.connection: It connects believers to the Annunciation, holy obedience, and divine comfort.

content.extended: The 19th day of each Ethiopian month remembers Archangel Gabriel and his ministry of holy announcement.

expandedContent.whyCelebrated: The 19th day of each Ethiopian month honors Archangel Gabriel, the messenger of divine glad tidings.
whatHappened: The Church remembers Gabriel's ministry of holy announcement and obedience to God.
His remembrance especially calls to mind the joy of divine message and God's saving intervention.
The day gives the faithful a recurring time to give thanks for God's messages of mercy and comfort.
expandedContent.significance: This commemoration keeps before believers the nearness of God's mercy and the angelic service that announces His will.

date.gregorianHint: Occurs on the 21st of every Ethiopian month

summary.short: Primary monthly commemoration of Saint Mary and her intercession.
summary.panel: The 21st day of each Ethiopian month is especially dedicated to Saint Mary.
summary.whyItMatters: It expresses the deep Marian devotion of the Church and trust in her intercession.
summary.connection: It connects the faithful to mercy, hope, purity, and the motherhood of Saint Mary.

content.extended: The 21st day of each Ethiopian month is especially dedicated to Saint Mary.

expandedContent.whyCelebrated: The 21st day of each Ethiopian month is a primary monthly remembrance of Saint Mary, the Mother of God.
whatHappened: The Church honors Saint Mary as the one who bore Christ our Saviour in the flesh.
The monthly remembrance keeps her intercession, humility, and obedience before the faithful.
Marian prayers and hymns help believers remember her place in the mystery of salvation.
expandedContent.significance: This commemoration expresses the Church's love for the Theotokos and teaches believers to follow her humility and faith.

date.gregorianHint: Occurs on the 23rd of every Ethiopian month

summary.short: Monthly commemoration of Saint Georgios, the victorious martyr.
summary.panel: The 23rd day of each Ethiopian month remembers Saint Georgios and his victorious witness.
summary.whyItMatters: It strengthens courage, faithfulness, and devotion to one of the Church’s beloved martyrs.
summary.connection: It connects the faithful to bold confession of Christ and steadfast endurance.

content.extended: The 23rd day of each Ethiopian month remembers Saint Georgios and his victorious witness.

expandedContent.whyCelebrated: The 23rd day of each Ethiopian month remembers Saint Georgios, the victorious martyr and beloved intercessor.
whatHappened: The Church remembers Saint Georgios for steadfast confession of Christ and courage under trial.
His monthly commemoration keeps the witness of martyrdom active in parish and household devotion.
The faithful ask his prayers and are encouraged to remain firm in faith.
expandedContent.significance: This remembrance strengthens courage, endurance, and trust in Christ when believers face hardship.

date.gregorianHint: Occurs on the 24th of every Ethiopian month

summary.short: Monthly commemoration of Abune Tekle Haymanot.
summary.panel: The 24th day of each Ethiopian month remembers Abune Tekle Haymanot, great Ethiopian monk and saint.
summary.whyItMatters: It keeps before the faithful a model of holiness, discipline, and Ethiopian spiritual heritage.
summary.connection: It connects the faithful to Ethiopian monastic witness and enduring intercession.

content.extended: The 24th day of each Ethiopian month remembers Abune Tekle Haymanot, great Ethiopian monk and saint.

expandedContent.whyCelebrated: The 24th day of each Ethiopian month remembers Abune Tekle Haymanot, one of Ethiopia's great saints and monastic fathers.
whatHappened: The Church honors his holiness, prayer, discipline, and missionary labor.
His memory is kept monthly because his witness is central to Ethiopian Orthodox spiritual heritage.
The commemoration invites believers to repentance, endurance, and disciplined prayer.
expandedContent.significance: This remembrance gives the faithful a model of ascetic devotion and love for the Church.

date.gregorianHint: Occurs on the 27th of every Ethiopian month

summary.short: Monthly commemoration of the Savior of the World.
summary.panel: The 27th day of each Ethiopian month remembers Medhane Alem, the Savior of the World.
summary.whyItMatters: It keeps the work of salvation and the mercy of Christ before the faithful every month.
summary.connection: It connects believers to redemption, divine mercy, and Christ the Savior.

content.extended: The 27th day of each Ethiopian month remembers Medhane Alem, the Savior of the World.

expandedContent.whyCelebrated: The 27th day of each Ethiopian month remembers Medhane Alem, Christ the Savior of the World.
whatHappened: The Church turns attention to the mercy and saving work of Christ.
This monthly remembrance keeps salvation, healing, and thanksgiving before the faithful.
It is observed with prayer and reverence for the Lord who saves His people.
expandedContent.significance: This commemoration centers monthly devotion on Christ's mercy and the salvation He gives to the world.

date.gregorianHint: Occurs on the 7th of every Ethiopian month

summary.short: Monthly commemoration of the Holy Trinity.
summary.panel: The 7th day of each Ethiopian month is kept in honor of the Holy Trinity: Father, Son, and Holy Spirit.
summary.whyItMatters: This monthly remembrance keeps worship centered on the one God worshiped in Trinity.
summary.connection: It connects daily prayer to the Church's confession of the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit.

content.extended: The 7th day of each Ethiopian month is kept in honor of the Holy Trinity: Father, Son, and Holy Spirit.

expandedContent.whyCelebrated: The 7th day of each Ethiopian month remembers the Holy Trinity, the one God worshiped as Father, Son, and Holy Spirit.
whatHappened: The Church marks the day with worship directed to the Holy Trinity.
This monthly remembrance keeps the faithful centered on the mystery of God's life and mercy.
The day is a recurring call to prayer, thanksgiving, and right worship.
expandedContent.significance: This commemoration anchors the calendar in the worship of the one God confessed in Trinity.

sources.title: Ethiopian Orthodox Tewahedo Church, Religious Holidays and Calendar

date.gregorianHint: Occurs on the 13th of every Ethiopian month

summary.panel: The 13th day of each Ethiopian month remembers Archangel Raphael, honored for healing and guidance by God's command.
summary.whyItMatters: This remembrance asks God for mercy, healing, and protection through the ministry of His angels.
summary.connection: It connects the faithful to the Church's angelic commemorations and prayers for healing.

content.extended: The 13th day of each Ethiopian month remembers Archangel Raphael, honored for healing and guidance by God's command.

expandedContent.whyCelebrated: The 13th day of each Ethiopian month remembers Archangel Raphael, associated in Church devotion with healing, mercy, and guidance.
whatHappened: The Church honors Raphael as a servant of God who ministers healing and help by divine command.
This monthly remembrance invites prayer for mercy, protection, and guidance.
The faithful keep the day with reverence, thanksgiving, and angelic hymns where customary.
expandedContent.significance: This commemoration points believers to God's care for body and soul through His holy angels.

date.gregorianHint: Occurs on the 16th of every Ethiopian month

summary.short: Monthly commemoration of Kidane Mihret, the Covenant of Mercy.
summary.panel: The 16th day of each Ethiopian month remembers Kidane Mihret, honoring the merciful promise associated with Saint Mary.
summary.whyItMatters: The commemoration points the faithful toward mercy, repentance, and the intercession of the Mother of God.
summary.connection: It connects the monthly calendar to the annual Kidane Mihret feast on Yekatit 16.

content.extended: The 16th day of each Ethiopian month remembers Kidane Mihret, honoring the merciful promise associated with Saint Mary.

expandedContent.whyCelebrated: The 16th day of each Ethiopian month remembers Kidane Mihret, the Covenant of Mercy, connected with devotion to Saint Mary and divine mercy.
whatHappened: The Church remembers the mercy of God and the intercession associated with the Mother of God.
This monthly observance calls believers to repentance, hope, and trust in mercy.
The day is often kept with Marian prayer, hymns, and acts of compassion.
expandedContent.significance: This commemoration teaches the faithful to seek mercy and to become merciful toward others.

sources.title: Ethiopian Orthodox Tewahedo Church, Religious Holidays and Calendar

date.gregorianHint: Occurs on the 22nd of every Ethiopian month

summary.panel: The 22nd day of each Ethiopian month remembers Archangel Uriel, honored as a minister of divine light and wisdom.
summary.whyItMatters: This remembrance calls the faithful to seek God's light, wisdom, and protection.
summary.connection: It connects the monthly cycle to the Church's reverence for the holy angels.

content.extended: The 22nd day of each Ethiopian month remembers Archangel Uriel, honored as a minister of divine light and wisdom.

expandedContent.whyCelebrated: The 22nd day of each Ethiopian month is kept in honor of Archangel Uriel and his intercession.
whatHappened: The Church remembers the works and intercession associated with Archangel Uriel.
This monthly remembrance helps the faithful keep his memory and seek his prayers.
The day points worshipers toward God's light, wisdom, and protection.
expandedContent.significance: This commemoration points believers to God's help through His holy angels.

date.gregorianHint: Occurs on the 25th of every Ethiopian month

summary.panel: The 25th day of each Ethiopian month remembers St. Merkorious, honored among the martyrs and warrior saints.
summary.whyItMatters: His remembrance encourages courage, steadfast faith, and trust in Christ under trial.
summary.connection: It connects monthly devotion with Synaxarium commemorations of St. Merkorious and churches dedicated in his honor.

content.extended: The 25th day of each Ethiopian month remembers St. Merkorious, honored among the martyrs and warrior saints.

expandedContent.whyCelebrated: The 25th day of each Ethiopian month remembers Saint Merkorious, a martyr honored for courage and steadfast faith.
whatHappened: The Church remembers Saint Merkorious as a faithful witness of Christ.
His monthly commemoration keeps the courage of the martyrs before the faithful.
The day invites believers to pray for endurance and firmness in confession.
expandedContent.significance: This remembrance encourages courage, patience, and trust in Christ under trial.

date.gregorianHint: Occurs on the 29th of every Ethiopian month

summary.short: Monthly remembrance of the Nativity of Christ.
summary.panel: The 29th day of each Ethiopian month remembers the birth of Christ, keeping the mystery of the Incarnation before the faithful.
summary.whyItMatters: This remembrance points to the Word of God made flesh for the salvation of the world.
summary.connection: It connects each month to the annual feast of Lidet/Gena on Tahsas 29.

content.extended: The 29th day of each Ethiopian month remembers the birth of Christ, keeping the mystery of the Incarnation before the faithful.

expandedContent.whyCelebrated: The 29th day of each Ethiopian month remembers the Nativity of Christ in a recurring monthly way.
whatHappened: The Church turns again to the mystery of the Word of God made flesh.
This remembrance keeps the joy and humility of Bethlehem before believers throughout the year.
The day calls the faithful to thanksgiving for the coming of the Savior.
expandedContent.significance: This commemoration points to the Incarnation and the mercy of Christ who entered the world for salvation.

sources.title: Ethiopian Orthodox Tewahedo Church, Religious Holidays and Calendar
